hints.text: Plates **black with two white sections** (visible through the blur); drives on the **left**

shared_with.note: Indonesian plates are black too — but with **three** white sections.
shared_with.diff: 2 sections → Malaysia, 3 → Indonesia. Also: Malaysia = all-white road lines, English-influenced spellings (televisyen).

hints.text: **Double white centre lines** on two-lane roads — the only SE-Asian country doing this
hints.uniq_note: Within Southeast Asia unique; double white centre lines exist outside the region, so pair with plates/language.

hints.text: Thick **yellow bars** on the road edge

shared_with.note: Yellow edge markings exist in the UK too.
shared_with.diff: European environment, UK plates → United Kingdom.

hints.text: Electricity poles with **black or white stickers** (sometimes red-white / red-blue stripes below the black sticker)
**BERHENTI** (stop) & **BERI LALUAN** (yield) signs
Bollards in **black/white/grey/red** combinations (several varieties, same layout)
Directional signs with **hexagonal yellow highway shields**; km markers **white-and-blue** (shields often visible on them)
**Malay** (Latin script) — practically indistinguishable from Indonesian for non-speakers

shared_with.diff: English-influenced spellings (**-syen**) → Malaysia; Dutch-influenced (**-si**) → Indonesia. Plates 2 vs 3 sections.

hints.text: **Chinese characters** are common (24.6% of the population is of Chinese descent) — unlike Indonesia
Signposts almost always **black-and-white striped**; B&W kerbs common
Each state has its **own road-number letter prefix** (federal territories excluded; Sabah has only A2 & A7 as A-roads)
**State flags** appear regularly — worth learning; **postal codes** are on most street signs
**Black pole stickers** → mainland (on Borneo only in Gen4); **white pole stickers** → only Sarawak (Borneo)
Black stickers with **'TEL TNB'** at the bottom — only in Johor; **yellow street-name signs with black outline** (S-Negeri-Sembilan: only the arrow tip black)
Lamp posts with **two small circles** on the support or **triangular metal support** → mainland Malaysia
Borneo poles: **2-1 insulator config + long diagonal wooden support** — exclusive to Sarawak (Indonesian lookalikes aren't wooden); 'boomerang' sheet-metal lamp posts (more outside Kuching)
Thin metal poles with **two parallel pole tops** — exclusive to Sabah; green arrow street signs with yellow **JKR** hexagon; very mountainous coverage (Sarawak = flatter, mountains only distant)
**Trash-bin logos** tell the state: 'S' logo → Negeri Sembilan/Malacca/Johor; leaf logo → Perlis/Kedah; 'Alam Flora' sunny landscape → Pahang/KL/Putrajaya
**Arrow-shaped street-name signs** (any colour) are exclusive to Malaysia
**Isolated rock formations** rising abruptly from flat land near the Thai border
Urban areas dotted with **blue cylindrical containers**
Unique **grey car with long front antenna** (Sarawak)
